properties.mechanism: Both metrics are derived from the same per-customer brand-preference model predictions, one sliced by value tier and the other aggregated across all customers, so they co-move.
Cross-brand co-affinity scores reflect the same underlying brand-preference signals that determine each tier's brand affinity, so the two move together.
Customers with strong, concentrated brand affinity are more likely to be classified as loyalists rather than explorers or switchers, so affinity drives the loyalty split.
Both metrics segment customers by the same value tiers, so tier-level brand affinity and tier-level channel affinity co-vary across the shared customer segments.
Both are outputs of the same brand-affinity model, so the overall customers-per-brand distribution co-moves with the per-value-tier brand preference breakdown.
The loyalist/explorer/switcher split is derived from the same brand-preference scores, so concentration in the brand distribution tracks the loyalty index.
Cross-brand co-affinity scores are computed from the same per-customer brand preferences that underlie the brand distribution, so the two move together.
Both are derived from the same per-customer brand-preference signal, so brands that dominate the preference distribution also appear most frequently in the high-scoring co-affinity pairs.
Customers classified as Explorers/Switchers exhibit affinity across multiple brands, so a higher switcher/explorer share co-moves with a denser, higher-scoring cross-brand affinity matrix.
Both models read off the same customer brand-preference data, so brand pairs with strong co-affinity tend to track the dominant brand preferences seen within value tiers.

Customers with high cross-brand co-affinity (buying across many brand pairs) get classified as Switchers/Explorers, while those with low cross-brand affinity are classified as Loyalists, so the cross-brand affinity pattern drives the loyalty split.
Loyalists concentrate their purchases on a single top brand while switchers spread across brands, so the loyalty split co-moves with how customers distribute across top brands.
Switchers are far more likely to respond to discounts than entrenched Loyalists, so a customer's loyalty classification causally shifts their predicted discount-response probability.

Both are sibling outputs of the same category-purchase-propensity model derived from per-customer category propensity scores, so categories with many high-propensity customers show both larger audience sizes and higher average confidence.
Both are built from the same model's max_category_prob assignments, so the distribution of peak category probabilities co-moves with how customers concentrate into top categories.
The features the propensity model gains the most from determine each customer's predicted top category, shaping which categories accumulate the largest audiences.

The heatmap averages the per-customer max_category_prob (the model's confidence), which is exactly the value whose histogram this metric reports, so the heatmap is computed from these confidence scores.
Both are produced by the same category-propensity model and share the per-customer top-category assignment, so the heatmap's category rows co-move with the audience sizes per category.
The model's most predictive features determine the category-propensity confidence scores that are averaged into the heatmap, so feature gain drives the heatmap values.
The top-gain features are what the category propensity model splits on to produce each customer's max_category_prob, so they drive the shape of the purchase-likelihood distribution.
Both are feature-gain rankings from sibling category-focused propensity models trained on overlapping customer features, so their top signals tend to co-move.
Both are aggregations of the same category-propensity model's max_category_prob/confidence values, so a higher-confidence distribution shifts the per-category-x-tier average confidence upward.
Both derive from the same predicted class-probability vector p, so a peaked distribution yields a high max_category_prob and a low normalized entropy (they move inversely in lockstep).
Confidence tiers are assigned from the model's uncertainty score, which is the inverse of max_category_prob, so the likelihood distribution and tier breakdown co-move.
The seasonality index is computed directly as actual_month revenue divided by average monthly revenue, so monthly revenue is a formula input to it.
Peak and trough seasonality indices per category are derived from the monthly revenue series, making monthly revenue a component of their computation.
Monthly total revenue across all categories is the sum of revenue across the individual offer types on the same ml platform.
Both aggregate order totals into monthly revenue figures from the same underlying transactions, so they reliably co-move.
The revenue forecast is modeled to track actual monthly revenue using the seasonality index, so the two series move together.
Year-over-year category revenue change is derived from the same monthly revenue figures, so it co-moves with monthly revenue.
The peak and trough index values are simply the maximum and minimum of the per-month seasonality index (actual_month / avg_monthly_revenue) computed in the heatmap.
Peak and trough seasonality indices per category are extracted from the same underlying monthly seasonality index, so the two metrics move together by construction.
YoY change per category is computed by differencing category revenue across years, drawn from the same monthly revenue figures.
Seasonal demand swings (actual_month vs average) shift category revenue levels period over period, driving the observed YoY growth or decline.
Shifts in a category's seasonal peak and trough timing between years change the year-over-year revenue comparison for that category.
The budget optimizer reads each channel's response curve (revenue at each spend level) as the core input to compute the recommended budget share per channel.
The recommendation allocates budget across channels by equalizing marginal ROI (additional revenue per additional dollar), making marginal ROI a direct input to the allocation.
Saturation is computed as % of optimal budget currently allocated, so it uses the recommended budget allocation as its denominator/reference.
Marginal ROI is the slope of the channel response curve, computed as the incremental revenue per added dollar derived from the revenue-at-each-spend-level curve.
Budget is shifted toward channels with the highest marginal ROI until marginal returns equalize, so marginal ROI drives the recommended budget share.
As a channel's spend approaches its saturation point, its marginal ROI declines toward zero, so saturation level and marginal ROI move inversely.
Marginal ROI is computed as the slope (derivative) of the channel response curve, so the additional revenue per additional dollar is read directly off the response curve at the current spend level.
The saturation point (% of optimal budget allocated) is derived from where the response curve flattens, so the shape of the response curve determines the saturation summary.
Recommended budget share per channel is produced by optimizing across the per-channel response curves to maximize total predicted revenue.
The saturation point (% of optimal budget allocated) is computed directly from the channel response curve, which defines the revenue-per-spend shape and thus the optimal spend level.
Saturation levels per channel drive the recommended budget share, since under-saturated channels are allocated more budget and over-saturated ones less.
Both are derived from the same response curve, so marginal ROI falls as a channel approaches its saturation point, making the two reliably co-move inversely.

The confidence tier is assigned directly from the per-prediction uncertainty score (H(p)/log(n_classes)) produced by the same category-affinity model, so the uncertainty distribution is the computational input to the tier.
Both derive from the same uncertainty threshold, and the confidence tier a customer falls into determines whether a Top-1 or Top-3 shortlist recommendation strategy is applied.
Stronger predictive signals (high information-gain features) sharpen the model's class probabilities, lowering uncertainty and shifting predictions into higher confidence tiers.
The Recommendation Strategy Split thresholds on the uncertainty score (Top-1 if uncertainty < 60%, else Top-3), so it is computed from this subject metric.
Stronger predictive signals in the category-affinity model sharpen the class probability distribution, lowering the entropy-based uncertainty score.
Higher feature predictive power lowers uncertainty below the 60% threshold, moving more customers into the Top-1 rather than Top-3 recommendation strategy.

Both the recommendation strategy split and the confidence tier breakdown are derived from the same prediction uncertainty score, so high-confidence predictions map to Top-1 strategy and lower tiers map to Top-3 shortlists.
The subject conditions the same per-customer preferred-channel assignments on value tier, so the overall channel preference distribution is the marginal component it is built from.
Both are channel-by-tier outputs of the same channel-affinity model, so the predicted channel mix and the model's confidence per channel x tier co-move.
The overall channel preference distribution is the aggregate of the per-value-tier channel distributions, so it breaks down into the by-tier components.
Both are outputs of the same channel-affinity model scoring each customer's preferred channel, so the per-channel customer counts and the per-channel confidence scores co-move.
Both are channel-by-value-tier views produced by the same ml-channel-affinity model, so the confidence heatmap co-moves with the channel distribution it scores.
The averaged model confidence is computed over the same channel-preference predictions that drive the channel distribution, so the two outputs of the ml-channel-affinity model reliably co-move.
Both are structurally identical 'avg confidence per dimension x tier' heatmaps from parallel ML affinity pipelines, so their confidence levels tend to track together across tiers.
The top-gain features are the inputs the discount response model relies on most to compute each customer's prob_discount_response, so they drive the shape of the response probability distribution.

Both feature-importance outputs are computed over largely the same customer feature set, so features that are predictive in the discount model tend to also rank highly in the category propensity model.
Both rank predictive power over a shared pool of customer behavioral features, so high-gain features tend to co-occur across the two models.
